report_metadata.cancer_type: Salivary gland cancer — high-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma

treatments.name: Total Parotidectomy + Neck Dissection (Levels II–IV) + Adjuvant IMRT (60–66 Gy)

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO and NCCN guidelines recommend surgery with neck dissection for high-grade MEC with nodal disease (N1), followed by adjuvant RT for high-grade, T3, and node-positive tumors. National Cancer Database analyses confirm improved survival with adjuvant RT (5-year OS 67.1% vs 60.6% with observation after surgery).

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS for high-grade MEC ranges 0–43% without adjuvant therapy. Adjuvant RT improves locoregional control to 89.1% at 5 years and provides OS benefit (HR 0.76, P=0.002) in high-risk disease. Stage III with complete resection and adjuvant RT achieves the best possible outcomes.

accessibility.rationale: Standard surgical and radiation procedures available at all major cancer centers. No special equipment beyond IMRT capability required.

safety_profile.rationale: Surgical risks include facial nerve injury (10–30% for total parotidectomy), Frey syndrome, first-bite syndrome, shoulder dysfunction. RT causes xerostomia, mucositis, dysphagia, osteoradionecrosis risk. Prior radiation to face/neck region increases toxicity risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal standard of care regardless of biomarker status. CRTC1-MAML2 fusion-negative status indicates aggressive biology supporting aggressive locoregional treatment. No biomarker-specific modification needed.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Surgical excision provides definitive local tumor control through complete resection with negative margins. Neck dissection addresses regional nodal metastasis. Adjuvant IMRT delivers conformal radiation to the tumor bed and draining lymphatics, targeting residual microscopic disease through DNA double-strand breaks while sparing surrounding normal tissue.

key_evidence.study_name: National Cancer Database Analysis — Adjuvant Radiation for Salivary Gland Malignancies

notable_side_effects: Xerostomia (significant with prior neck radiation history)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — universally available at all cancer centers

treatments.name: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan (T-DXd / Enhertu)

evidence_level.rationale: MYTHOS Phase II trial showed 68.4% ORR in HER2-positive salivary gland cancer (19 patients). DESTINY-PanTumor02 demonstrated 61.3% ORR in HER2 IHC 3+ tumors across histologies. FDA approved for HER2+ solid tumors (tumor-agnostic approval April 2024) based on DESTINY-PanTumor02.

survival_benefit.rationale: MYTHOS trial: median PFS 15.9 months. DESTINY-PanTumor02 in IHC 3+: median PFS 11.9 months, median OS 21.1 months, median DOR 22.1 months. Substantial for a rare cancer with historically poor prognosis.

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved April 2024 for all HER2+ solid tumors (IHC 3+) under tumor-agnostic indication based on DESTINY-PanTumor02. Commercially available at oncology centers with infusion capability.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant ILD/pneumonitis risk (36.8% in MYTHOS trial, 10.5% overall in DP02, including 3 deaths). Grade 3/4 neutropenia 31.6%, lymphopenia 15.8%, anemia 15.8%. Nausea common. Requires careful ILD monitoring.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is HER2 amplified — direct molecular target. T-DXd delivers cytotoxic payload directly to HER2-expressing cells. Tumor-agnostic approval specifically covers HER2 IHC 3+ solid tumors.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Antibody-drug conjugate comprising trastuzumab linked to deruxtecan (DXd), a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload, via a cleavable tetrapeptide linker. Binds HER2 on tumor surface, undergoes receptor-mediated internalization, and releases cytotoxic DXd payload intracellularly. High drug-to-antibody ratio (DAR ~8) and bystander killing effect on neighboring HER2-low cells.

key_evidence.study_name: MYTHOS Phase II Trial — T-DXd in HER2+ Salivary Gland Cancer

biomarker_requirements: HER2 IHC 3+ or HER2 amplification by FISH
notable_side_effects: Interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis (36.8% in SGC; can be fatal)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved — tumor-agnostic HER2+ solid tumors (April 2024)

treatments.name: Adjuvant Trastuzumab + Carboplatin/Paclitaxel Concurrent with RT, Followed by Maintenance Trastuzumab

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective study of 95 patients showed adjuvant trastuzumab dramatically improved DFS (117 vs 9 months, P=0.02) and OS (74 vs 43 months, P=0.02) in HER2 3+ salivary gland cancers. None of six HER2 2–3+ patients on adjuvant therapy recurred. Dana-Farber Phase II trial (NCT04620187) ongoing.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median DFS of 117 months vs 9 months (P=0.02) and median OS of 74 months vs 43 months (P=0.02) in HER2 3+ patients receiving adjuvant trastuzumab. Recurrence rate only 22% with trastuzumab vs much higher without.

accessibility.rationale: Trastuzumab is FDA-approved and widely available. Carboplatin/paclitaxel are standard agents. Use in salivary gland cancer is off-label but supported by NCCN guidelines and emerging evidence.

safety_profile.rationale: Cardiotoxicity risk with trastuzumab (2–4% symptomatic CHF). Carboplatin causes myelosuppression, nausea. Paclitaxel causes neuropathy, alopecia. Combined with RT increases acute mucositis and dysphagia.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is HER2 amplified — directly targets the overexpressed HER2 receptor. HER2 3+ salivary gland cancers show the greatest benefit from adjuvant trastuzumab therapy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trastuzumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that binds HER2 extracellular domain IV, inhibiting receptor dimerization, triggering antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), and blocking downstream PI3K/AKT and MAPK signaling. Combined with platinum-taxane chemotherapy and radiation for multimodal attack on HER2-amplified cancer cells.

key_evidence.study_name: The Benefits of Adjuvant Trastuzumab for HER-2-Positive Salivary Gland Cancers

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for salivary gland cancer) — widely available

treatments.name: Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab (Dual HER2 Blockade)

evidence_level.rationale: MyPathway Phase IIa basket trial: 15 HER2-amplified SGC patients showed 60% ORR (1 CR, 8 PR), including 4 patients with high-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Median DOR 9.2 months. Chemotherapy-free regimen.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 8.6 months, median OS 20.4 months in HER2+ SGC patients. Durable responses with 5 of 9 responders still responding at data cutoff. For a rare aggressive cancer, these represent meaningful outcomes.

accessibility.rationale: Both trastuzumab and pertuzumab are FDA-approved HER2 agents (approved for breast/gastric cancer). Off-label use for SGC supported by MyPathway data. Commercially available at oncology infusion centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Well-tolerated chemotherapy-free regimen. Grade 3–4 TEAEs in only 25%. Most common AEs: diarrhea (50%), pruritus (31%), chills (25%). No grade 4 drug-related TEAEs. Zero treatment-related deaths. Favorable vs trastuzumab+docetaxel (>60% grade 4 neutropenia).

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets HER2 — patient is HER2 amplified. Pertuzumab blocks HER2 dimerization (domain II) while trastuzumab blocks domain IV, providing complementary dual blockade of the HER2 receptor.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual HER2 blockade: trastuzumab binds HER2 subdomain IV preventing ligand-independent signaling and triggering ADCC; pertuzumab binds subdomain II blocking HER2/HER3 dimerization. Together they provide more complete suppression of HER2 downstream signaling (PI3K/AKT, MAPK) than either agent alone.

key_evidence.study_name: MyPathway Phase IIa Basket Study — Salivary Gland Carcinoma Cohort

biomarker_requirements: HER2 amplification or overexpression (IHC 3+ or FISH+)
treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for SGC) — commercially available

treatments.name: Trastuzumab + Docetaxel

evidence_level.rationale: Multicenter Phase II trial (Japan) of 57 patients with HER2+ salivary gland cancer showed 70.2% ORR and 14% CR. NCCN category 2A recommendation for HER2+ SGC. Most studied HER2-chemo combination in this setting.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 39.7 months in the Japanese Phase II trial — among the longest reported for metastatic/recurrent HER2+ SGC. Median PFS 8.5 months. Durable responses in a subset of patients.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved and widely available. Standard oncology infusion centers can administer. Off-label for SGC but NCCN-listed.

safety_profile.rationale: Significant toxicity: >60% grade 4 neutropenia reported. Docetaxel causes myelosuppression, peripheral neuropathy, fluid retention. Trastuzumab adds cardiotoxicity risk. G-CSF support typically required.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets HER2 amplification — patient's confirmed biomarker. HER2+ SGC patients show the best response rates to trastuzumab-containing regimens.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Trastuzumab binds HER2 extracellular domain, blocking downstream signaling and triggering ADCC. Docetaxel stabilizes microtubules, preventing mitotic cell division. The combination exploits HER2-dependent survival signaling while independently attacking cell division machinery.

key_evidence.study_name: Multicenter Phase II Trial of Trastuzumab and Docetaxel for HER2-Positive Salivary Gland Cancer

treatments.availability: FDA Approved (off-label for SGC) — NCCN Category 2A recommended

treatments.name: Combined Androgen Blockade (Leuprorelin + Bicalutamide)

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective Phase II study of 36 AR-positive SGC patients showed 41.7% ORR and 75% clinical benefit rate. Japanese study reported 75% CBR with 30.5-month median OS. Dutch study of 35 patients showed 50% clinical benefit. Multiple retrospective series support activity.

survival_benefit.rationale: Japanese study: median PFS 8.8 months, median OS 30.5 months. Dutch study: median OS 17 months. Retrospective series: 64.7% ORR, 11.8% 3-year PFS. Adjuvant ADT improved 3-year DFS from 27.7% to 48.2% (P=0.037) in stage IVA disease.

accessibility.rationale: Leuprorelin and bicalutamide are widely available, FDA-approved (for prostate cancer), and inexpensive generic options exist. Oral bicalutamide plus subcutaneous/intramuscular leuprorelin — easy to administer.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated hormonal therapy. Side effects include hot flashes, fatigue, loss of libido, osteoporosis risk with prolonged use, gynecomastia, hepatotoxicity (rare with bicalutamide). Much better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is AR positive — directly targets androgen receptor signaling. Combined androgen blockade provides both peripheral AR blockade (bicalutamide) and central testosterone suppression (leuprorelin) for comprehensive AR pathway inhibition.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual androgen deprivation: leuprorelin (GnRH agonist) suppresses testicular testosterone production via pituitary desensitization; bicalutamide (non-steroidal antiandrogen) competitively blocks the androgen receptor, preventing nuclear translocation and transcriptional activation of AR-dependent genes driving tumor growth.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Combined Androgen Blockade in AR-Positive Salivary Gland Carcinoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for prostate cancer (off-label for SGC) — widely available

treatments.name: Ado-Trastuzumab Emtansine (T-DM1 / Kadcyla)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II basket trial reported up to 90% ORR in HER2+ salivary gland tumors previously treated with trastuzumab. NCI-MATCH trial (EAY131 subprotocol Q) showed partial responses in SGC including mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Multiple case reports confirm activity. MSK Phase II trial ongoing.

survival_benefit.rationale: Limited long-term survival data specific to SGC. Case reports demonstrate durable responses including resolution of liver, bone, and skin metastases. Active as second-line after trastuzumab progression. Dana-Farber adjuvant trial ongoing.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for HER2+ breast cancer. Off-label use for SGC. Available at major oncology centers. Multiple ongoing clinical trials provide access pathways.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than trastuzumab+docetaxel. Lower rates of severe neutropenia. Main toxicities: thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), fatigue, nausea. Relatively low toxicity in case reports.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets HER2 — patient is HER2 amplified. Delivers cytotoxic DM1 (maytansinoid) payload specifically to HER2-overexpressing cells via antibody-directed internalization.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Antibody-drug conjugate combining trastuzumab with emtansine (DM1), a potent maytansinoid microtubule inhibitor, via a non-cleavable thioether linker (MCC). Binds HER2 on tumor cell surface, internalizes via receptor-mediated endocytosis, and releases DM1 intracellularly to disrupt microtubule assembly and induce cell death.

key_evidence.study_name: NCI-MATCH Trial (EAY131) Subprotocol Q — T-DM1 in HER2-Amplified Tumors

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for breast cancer (off-label for SGC) — clinical trials available

treatments.name: Cisplatin + Docetaxel

evidence_level.rationale: Prospective Phase II trial of 41 patients with metastatic/recurrent SGC showed 46.3% ORR (7.3% CR) overall, with 86.6% ORR in non-ACC subtypes (including MEC and ductal carcinoma). Well-designed single-arm study with clear endpoints.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 9.4 months, median OS 28.2 months overall. Non-ACC subtypes: median PFS 10.5 months, median OS 29.3 months. Meaningful survival in a disease with limited systemic options, though no randomized comparison available.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and docetaxel are generic, inexpensive, and available worldwide. Standard chemotherapy agents at all oncology centers. No special infrastructure required.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ neutropenia 4.9%, fatigue 4.9%. Alopecia 61%, anorexia 43.9%, diarrhea 31.7%. Cisplatin nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity. Docetaxel neuropathy and fluid retention. No treatment-related deaths. Manageable with supportive care.

biomarker_match.rationale: Non-biomarker-directed cytotoxic therapy. Not specific to CRTC1-MAML2, HER2, or AR status. However, non-ACC salivary subtypes (including MEC) showed dramatically higher ORR (86.6%) than ACC (23.1%), suggesting relative chemosensitivity.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms intra- and inter-strand DNA crosslinks, preventing DNA replication and transcription, triggering apoptosis. Docetaxel binds to beta-tubulin, stabilizing microtubules and preventing their depolymerization, arresting cells in mitosis. The combination attacks two independent cellular processes for synergistic cytotoxicity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Cisplatin Plus Weekly Docetaxel in Metastatic/Recurrent SGC

treatments.name: Enzalutamide

evidence_level.rationale: Alliance A091404 Phase II trial enrolled 46 AR+ SGC patients. Failed primary endpoint with only 4.3% confirmed ORR (2 PR). However, 52.2% stable disease, 56.5% tumor regression, and 39.1% had >=6-month PFS, suggesting disease-stabilizing activity.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 5.6 months, median OS 17.0 months. Three patients remained on treatment at 32–50 months, indicating durable benefit in a subset. Clinical activity as monotherapy is modest but provides a chemotherapy-free option.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved oral agent (for prostate cancer). Off-label for SGC. Available at pharmacies. Convenient once-daily oral dosing (160 mg). No infusion required.

safety_profile.rationale: Fatigue (89%), hypertension (74%), hot flashes (48%), weight loss (46%). Grade 3+ related AEs: hypertension and fatigue (9% each). Manageable but high rates of fatigue and hypertension may affect quality of life.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is AR positive — enzalutamide is a potent next-generation antiandrogen. Blocks AR ligand binding, nuclear translocation, and co-activator recruitment. More potent AR inhibition than bicalutamide, though CAB showed better ORR in SGC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Second-generation androgen receptor inhibitor that competitively binds the AR ligand-binding domain with higher affinity than bicalutamide. Inhibits AR nuclear translocation, DNA binding, and co-activator recruitment. Unlike first-generation antiandrogens, does not exhibit partial agonist activity.

key_evidence.study_name: Alliance A091404 — Phase II Study of Enzalutamide for AR+ Salivary Gland Cancers

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for prostate cancer (off-label for SGC) — oral, widely available

treatments.name: Abiraterone Acetate + Prednisone

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial of 23 ADT-resistant AR+ SGC patients showed 21% ORR and 62.5% disease control rate. Provides option after failure of combined androgen blockade. Limited sample size but prospective data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 3.7 months, median OS 22.4 months. Modest PFS but reasonable OS, suggesting some patients derive extended benefit. Useful as second-line AR-targeted therapy after CAB progression.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for castration-resistant prostate cancer. Generic abiraterone available. Oral dosing. Off-label for SGC but rationale well-established.

safety_profile.rationale: Requires concurrent prednisone to prevent mineralocorticoid excess. Hypertension, hypokalemia, fluid retention, hepatotoxicity. Generally well-tolerated in prostate cancer setting. Less toxic than chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Patient is AR positive. Abiraterone inhibits CYP17A1, blocking androgen synthesis at adrenal and intratumoral levels — complementary mechanism to AR antagonists. Addresses androgen production rather than just receptor blockade.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Irreversible inhibitor of CYP17A1 (17alpha-hydroxylase/C17,20 lyase), blocking androgen biosynthesis at adrenal glands and within tumor tissue. Reduces circulating and intratumoral androgen levels below those achieved by GnRH agonists alone. Combined with low-dose prednisone to prevent mineralocorticoid excess from CYP17A1 inhibition.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Abiraterone Acetate in ADT-Resistant AR+ Salivary Gland Carcinoma

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for prostate cancer (off-label for SGC) — generic available

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab (Anti-PD-1 Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-028 Phase 1b (26 PD-L1+ SGC patients): 12% ORR. KEYNOTE-158 Phase II (109 patients): 5% ORR. One documented complete pathological response in mucoepidermoid carcinoma. MEC has immune-inflamed phenotype, potentially more responsive than other SGC subtypes.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-028: median OS 13 months, median PFS 4 months. KEYNOTE-158: median OS 21.1 months, median PFS 4 months. Modest activity overall but occasional deep/durable responses. Not recommended outside clinical trials for most SGC patients.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for multiple indications including MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors and TMB-high tumors (tumor-agnostic). Widely available. Would need TMB or PD-L1 testing for labeled use, or off-label for SGC.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well-tolerated. Immune-related AEs include thyroiditis, pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, skin reactions — typically manageable with corticosteroids. Better safety profile than cytotoxic chemotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not directly targeting HER2 or AR — patient's primary actionable biomarkers. Efficacy depends on PD-L1 expression and TMB (not assessed in this patient). MEC's immune-inflamed phenotype may favor checkpoint inhibitor response vs other SGC subtypes.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction, restoring T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. By preventing tumor cells from engaging the PD-1 immune checkpoint on T cells, pembrolizumab releases the 'brakes' on adaptive immune responses against tumor neoantigens.

key_evidence.study_name: KEYNOTE-028 Phase 1b — Pembrolizumab in Advanced SGC

biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR or TMB-high for tumor-agnostic approval

treatments.name: Nivolumab + Ipilimumab (Dual Checkpoint Blockade)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial of 64 patients with metastatic SGC. Non-ACC cohort met primary endpoint with 16% ORR (vs 6% in ACC). Limited but prospective data. Full results expected mid-2025.

survival_benefit.rationale: ORR of 16% in non-ACC SGC. Survival data still maturing. Dual checkpoint blockade may provide deeper and more durable responses than anti-PD-1 monotherapy in the small responder subset.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved for multiple cancers. Off-label for SGC. Available at major cancer centers. Cost and insurance coverage may be barriers for off-label dual immunotherapy.

safety_profile.rationale: Higher toxicity than pembrolizumab alone. Dual checkpoint blockade associated with grade 3+ immune-related AEs in 30–50% of patients across indications. Colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies more frequent.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not targeting patient's primary biomarkers (HER2, AR). MEC's immune-inflamed phenotype may favor response. No validated predictive biomarkers for dual checkpoint blockade in SGC established.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab blocks PD-1 on T cells, releasing adaptive immune checkpoint inhibition. Ipilimumab blocks CTLA-4, enhancing T cell priming and activation in lymph nodes. The combination targets two non-redundant immune checkpoints, providing synergistic anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 Trial of Nivolumab Plus Ipilimumab in Advanced Salivary Gland Cancer

treatments.availability: FDA Approved for multiple cancers (off-label for SGC) — available at major centers

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Vorinostat (HDAC Inhibitor + Immunotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II trial of 25 patients with recurrent/metastatic SGC showed 16% ORR. Vorinostat (HDAC inhibitor) hypothesized to enhance immunotherapy response through epigenetic modulation of the tumor microenvironment.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median PFS 6.9 months and median OS 14 months. Modest improvement over pembrolizumab monotherapy (12% ORR, 13-month OS in KEYNOTE-028). Combination did not dramatically change the outlook.

accessibility.rationale: Vorinostat is FDA-approved for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. Off-label for SGC. Pembrolizumab widely available. Combination requires off-label access to both agents for this indication.

safety_profile.rationale: HDAC inhibitor adds fatigue, thrombocytopenia, nausea, diarrhea to immune-related AEs from pembrolizumab. Manageable but more toxic than pembrolizumab alone.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not targeting patient's key biomarkers (HER2, AR). Epigenetic modulation is broadly acting. No validated predictive biomarker for this combination in SGC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Vorinostat inhibits histone deacetylases (HDACs), leading to chromatin remodeling, re-expression of silenced tumor suppressor genes, and upregulation of MHC class I molecules and tumor antigens. This epigenetic priming enhances tumor immunogenicity and synergizes with pembrolizumab's PD-1 blockade to restore anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Trial of Vorinostat + Pembrolizumab in Recurrent/Metastatic SGC

treatments.availability: Off-label — both agents FDA approved for other indications

treatments.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase II study of 26 evaluable patients with R/M salivary gland cancers. Trial met primary endpoint for best overall response. However, significant safety concerns emerged with 4 possibly treatment-related deaths, particularly in salivary duct carcinoma patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Trial was positive for the primary endpoint but details not fully published. Earlier ACC cohort failed to show superiority over lenvatinib alone. High-grade toxicity events including fatal outcomes temper the survival analysis.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents FDA-approved for other indications (lenvatinib for thyroid/HCC/endometrial; pembrolizumab for multiple cancers). Off-label combination for SGC. Available at major oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Serious safety concerns: 4 possibly treatment-related deaths (respiratory failure, cardiac arrest, stroke). 71% grade 3+ AEs. High rate of treatment interruptions and dose reductions. Significant concern for SDC histology specifically.

biomarker_match.rationale: Not targeting HER2 or AR directly. Lenvatinib provides broad VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR/RET/KIT inhibition. Pembrolizumab adds immune checkpoint blockade. Mechanism is not biomarker-matched to this patient's molecular profile.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lenvatinib is a multi-kinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR1-3, FGFR1-4, PDGFRalpha, RET, and KIT, suppressing tumor angiogenesis and growth signaling. Pembrolizumab blocks PD-1 immune checkpoint. The combination leverages lenvatinib's immunomodulatory effects (reducing regulatory T cells, enhancing antigen presentation) to synergize with checkpoint inhibition.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib Plus Pembrolizumab in R/M Salivary Gland Cancers

notable_side_effects: Fatal events reported (respiratory failure, cardiac arrest, stroke)
treatments.availability: Off-label — both agents FDA approved for other indications; clinical trial access

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Study of Adjuvant Ado-Trastuzumab Emtansine (T-DM1) for HER2-Positive Salivary Gland Carcinoma
sites: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, New York, NY
clinical_trials.early_results: First clinical trial exploring early addition of concurrent and adjuvant HER2-directed therapy with standard chemoradiation in high-risk HER2+ SGC. Enrollment ongoing; no interim results reported.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in HER2-Positive Recurrent/Metastatic Salivary Gland Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: ORR 68.4% (13/19) by blinded independent review. Median PFS 15.9 months. Grade 3/4 neutropenia 31.6%, ILD/pneumonitis 36.8%. Supports T-DXd as effective HER2-targeted option in SGC.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Lenvatinib and Pembrolizumab in Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma and Salivary Gland Cancer
clinical_trials.early_results: Met primary endpoint for best overall response in 26 evaluable SGC patients. However, significant safety concerns with 4 possibly treatment-related deaths (respiratory failure, cardiac arrest, stroke). 71% grade 3+ AEs.

clinical_trials.title: Efficacy and Safety of Trastuzumab Deruxtecan in Patients With HER2-Expressing Solid Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Overall ORR 37.1%, but IHC 3+ subgroup: 61.3% ORR, 11.9-month median PFS, 21.1-month median OS, 22.1-month median DOR. Led to tumor-agnostic FDA approval. Includes head & neck/salivary gland patients in 'other tumors' cohort.

clinical_trials.title: MyPathway Phase IIa Multiple Basket Study — HER2+ Salivary Gland Carcinoma Cohort
biomarker_requirements: HER2 amplification, HER2 overexpression, or HER2 mutation
clinical_trials.early_results: 15 HER2-amplified SGC patients treated with pertuzumab + trastuzumab achieved 60% ORR (1 CR, 8 PR), 8.6-month median PFS, 20.4-month median OS. 4 patients had high-grade mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Chemotherapy-free regimen with favorable tolerability.

clinical_trials.title: Phase II Study of Enzalutamide for Patients With Androgen Receptor-Positive Salivary Gland Cancers
clinical_trials.early_results: 46 patients enrolled. Failed primary endpoint (ORR 4.3%). However, 52.2% stable disease, 56.5% any tumor regression, median PFS 5.6 months, median OS 17.0 months. Three patients remained on treatment at 32–50 months. Enzalutamide alone insufficient but disease-stabilizing.

clinical_trials.title: Radiation Therapy With or Without Cisplatin in Treating Patients With High-Risk Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors That Have Been Removed By Surgery
sites: NRG Oncology / RTOG network — multi-center US
clinical_trials.early_results: Evaluating whether adding concurrent cisplatin to adjuvant RT improves outcomes in resected high-risk SGC (high-grade, positive margins, nodal involvement, PNI, T3-T4). Results pending. Prior retrospective data showed no clear benefit from adding chemotherapy to adjuvant RT.

combination_strategies.rationale: Patient co-expresses HER2 amplification and AR positivity. Expert consensus favors initiating HER2-targeted therapy first (ORR 71.2% vs CAB 13.0% in head-to-head comparison). Sequential or concurrent dual targeting of HER2 and AR pathways may provide complementary tumor control. No prospective trial of simultaneous HER2+AR blockade completed, but clinical rationale is strong for this dual-positive profile.

ADC-induced immunogenic cell death may enhance neoantigen presentation and prime anti-tumor immune responses. T-DXd's bystander killing effect expands the immune target repertoire. Combination being explored in breast cancer (BEGONIA trial). MEC's immune-inflamed microenvironment may be particularly amenable to ADC+ICI synergy.

Adding HER2-targeted therapy concurrently with standard adjuvant chemoradiation aims to address both locoregional and distant disease control. HER2-amplified SGC has high risk of distant metastasis. Trastuzumab concurrent with RT has radiosensitizing properties. Maintenance trastuzumab extends systemic HER2 suppression. Being evaluated at Dana-Farber and MSK.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase II data (extrapolated from breast cancer + SGC cohorts)
combination_strategies.rationale: Dual HER2 blockade provides more complete suppression of HER2 signaling than trastuzumab alone. Adding docetaxel increases cytotoxic effect. In breast cancer, pertuzumab + trastuzumab + docetaxel (CLEOPATRA regimen) dramatically improved OS. MyPathway showed dual HER2 blockade alone achieved 60% ORR, potentially higher with chemotherapy addition.

For patients with dual HER2+/AR+ profile who progress on HER2-directed therapy, combining next-generation AR inhibition with HER2-targeted ADC may attack both oncogenic drivers simultaneously. Cross-talk between HER2 and AR signaling pathways has been documented in SGC. Combined inhibition may prevent resistance through pathway switching.

supportive_care.approach: Xerostomia Management (Saliva Substitutes + Pilocarpine + Oral Hygiene Program)
supportive_care.evidence: ISOO/MASCC/ASCO Guideline on Salivary Gland Hypofunction and Xerostomia (JCO 2021). Intermediate-quality evidence supports topical mucosal lubricants, saliva substitutes, and sialagogues. Patient's prior neck radiation plus planned adjuvant RT creates high cumulative xerostomia risk.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces dry mouth symptoms, prevents dental caries and oral infections, improves swallowing and speech, enhances quality of life. Pilocarpine can stimulate residual salivary function. IMRT with parotid-sparing technique reduces severity.

supportive_care.approach: Facial Nerve Rehabilitation (Physical Therapy + Neuromuscular Retraining)
supportive_care.evidence: Post-parotidectomy facial nerve dysfunction occurs in 10–30% of cases. Nerve grafting may be required if facial nerve is sacrificed. Physical therapy and neuromuscular retraining can restore function. Speech and swallowing therapy addresses chewing/swallowing deficits.
supportive_care.benefit: Restores facial muscle function and symmetry, improves eye closure (prevents corneal exposure), restores oral competence for eating and drinking, improves social functioning and psychological wellbeing.

supportive_care.evidence: Dysphagia affects 30–60% of head and neck cancer patients post-treatment. Prophylactic swallowing exercises initiated before RT reduce long-term stricture risk. Enteral feeding (PEG) may be needed during acute treatment phase. Dietitian-guided nutritional optimization improves treatment tolerance.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintains nutritional status during treatment, prevents weight loss and muscle wasting, reduces aspiration risk, improves treatment tolerance and completion rates, preserves long-term swallowing function.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — NCCN Head and Neck Cancer Supportive Care Guidelines

supportive_care.approach: Cardiac Monitoring Program (Echocardiography for HER2-Targeted Therapy)
supportive_care.evidence: Trastuzumab-based therapies carry 2–4% risk of symptomatic CHF and up to 10–15% asymptomatic LVEF decline. ESMO/AHA guidelines recommend baseline echocardiography and every 3 months during HER2-targeted therapy. T-DXd also requires ILD monitoring with chest CT.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of cardiotoxicity allows dose modification or treatment change before irreversible damage. LVEF decline is often reversible if detected early. Enables safe continuation of life-extending HER2-targeted therapy.

supportive_care.approach: Psychological Support and Head/Neck Cancer Survivorship Program
supportive_care.evidence: Head and neck cancer patients experience depression (20–40%), anxiety, body image disturbance, and social isolation. Young patients (age 45) face additional concerns about career impact and relationships. Multidisciplinary survivorship programs with psycho-oncology, social work, and peer support improve outcomes.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduces depression and anxiety, improves treatment adherence, addresses body image and social functioning concerns, enhances overall quality of life, provides peer support network for rare cancer patients.

supportive_care.approach: Bone Health Management (for Androgen Deprivation Therapy)
supportive_care.evidence: If CAB is initiated, long-term androgen deprivation increases osteoporosis risk (10–15% bone density loss in 2 years). DEXA screening, calcium/vitamin D supplementation, and denosumab or bisphosphonate prophylaxis recommended per ASCO guidelines for men on ADT.
supportive_care.benefit: Prevents osteoporotic fractures, maintains bone mineral density during hormonal therapy, enables safe long-term use of AR-targeted therapy which may provide ongoing disease control.
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Strong — ASCO Bone Health Guidelines for Patients on ADT
